opp.ais_why:
• PBF issued a formal Source-to-Pay RFP for a 'tech-enabled Procurement Operations transformation'; Agentic AI, Merlin and Merlin Intake are scoped, so AI capability is in play as part of the platform.
• The stated driver is broad S2P modernization across sourcing, contracting and operational spend rather than AI specifically; AI is not called out as the primary evaluation criterion.
• Largest deal in the set ($823K) at Formal Evaluation, but no agentic-platform discussion or AI-requirement rating is recorded, so appetite reads as open-but-unproven.

metrics.narrative: PBF Energy operates at significant scale: $2.5-3.0B annual spend (CapEx $900M, OpEx $1.6-2.5B), 168,000 invoices/year, 40-50K POs and PRs annually, 3,800 employees, 70 procurement power users, and 2,300 casual/self-service users across 1,285 active suppliers and 2,335 active contracts (1,435 procurement, 900 commercial). The business problem articulated on the August 2025 intro call was operational inefficiency and lack of visibility across Source-to-Pay, with Diane Dunbar stating 'our current processes are fragmented and we need better reporting and compliance tracking' and noting 'the team spends too much time on manual tasks that should be automated.' The reorganization (Diane Dunbar, Aug 2025) and mandate to deliver cost savings imply a quantified ROI target, but no specific savings figure or cycle-time reduction has been stated. The $823K investment scale and 20 annual sourcing events (10 complex) suggest procurement is strategic, not transactional. Quantifying the ROI case (savings target, headcount impact, cycle-time reduction) remains a priority action for the post-demo feedback call.

champion.narrative: Diane Dunbar (Sr. Procurement Coordinator, IT) is the primary Zycus contact and RFP lead, demonstrating procedural engagement (coordinating the RFP, attending the Aug 2025 intro call, requesting the NDA and RFP template, confirming Zycus intent to respond), but has not yet demonstrated advocacy. She has not introduced Zycus to the Economic Buyer, has not provided competitive intelligence (shortlist unknown), has not pushed back against competitors on Zycus's behalf, and has not driven urgency beyond the RFP timeline. Her role is coordinator/evaluator, not decision-maker, and her access to the EB/DM is unknown. Kelsey Mckell (Procurement Manager) is the highest-ranking buyer contact on record but has been passive since the Aug 2025 call (no follow-up engagement, no tasks, no demonstrated advocacy). Archland Bergeron (Sr. Procurement Coordinator) is a third contact role but has shown no activity. The champion gap is that Zycus has no one actively selling internally, no one providing intel on the competition or the decision-maker's priorities, and no one escalating on Zycus's behalf. The post-demo feedback call (week of 29 Jun 2026) and the conference-room demos (~13 Jul) are the critical windows to convert Diane to active champion (by demonstrating Zycus solves her stated pain better than competitors and offering to help her build the internal business case) or to identify a different champion (Kelsey Mckell if she re-engages, or a sourcing/contracts power-user if demos surface one).

sources: Avoma intro call Aug 2025, Diane Dunbar (SAP confirmed: 'We are evaluating options and SAP is obviously one we're looking at given our ERP')
competition.narrative: SAP is confirmed as a competing vendor, named by Diane Dunbar on the August 2025 intro call when discussing PBF's SAP ECC environment and future S/4HANA migration plans ('We are evaluating options and SAP is obviously one we're looking at given our ERP'). The competitive threat is SAP's native integration advantage (Ariba or S/4HANA procurement modules integrate seamlessly with the relevant stakeholder, eliminating the third-party integration risk Zycus carries) and incumbency (PBF already runs SAP ECC, so SAP sales likely has existing relationships at the IT and Finance level). However, SAP's weakness is the lack of best-of-breed procurement depth (sourcing sophistication, contract lifecycle management, AI/Merlin-level automation) compared to the relevant stakeholder, the competitive shortlist is unknown. The formal RFP process and $823K deal size imply a multi-vendor bake-off (likely including Coupa, Jaggaer, or GEP based on typical enterprise Source-to-Pay competitions), but Zycus has no visibility into who else is shortlisted, who is ahead, or what differentiation angles competitors are using. This blind-spot risk means Zycus may be unknowingly trailing a frontrunner or may invest demo time in the wrong features. The immediate move is to ask Diane on the post-demo feedback call (week of 29 Jun): 'Who else is in the running, and what are you seeing as each vendor's strengths and gaps so far?' Then tailor the 13 Jul conference-room demo to win the head-to-head, emphasizing Zycus's sourcing depth, contract management, and Merlin AI as differentiators against SAP's integration-only pitch and any competitor's weaker AI story.

identify_pain.narrative: The operational pain is fragmented Source-to-Pay processes, lack of spend visibility, and manual work consuming procurement team capacity. On the August 2025 intro call, Diane Dunbar stated: 'Our current processes are fragmented and we need better reporting and compliance tracking' and 'The team spends too much time on manual tasks that should be automated.' The pain owner is the procurement organization (70 power users, 2,300 casual users), with Diane coordinating the procurement-technology workstream and Kelsey Mckell (Procurement Manager) likely owning procurement operations. The reorganization mentioned by Diane (Aug 2025) and the mandate to deliver cost savings imply the pain is strategic (executive-driven transformation) rather than purely tactical. The scale of the pain is visible in the metrics: 168K invoices/year, 40-50K POs and PRs annually, 1,285 suppliers, 2,335 active contracts, and 20 sourcing events/year (10 complex) — all managed with 'fragmented' systems. The pain is confirmed and quantified at the operational level; what remains unconfirmed is the financial impact (cost of manual work, savings opportunity, error/compliance cost) and whether the pain is urgent enough to force the December close or whether the timeline is aspirational.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are partially visible. On the August 2025 intro call, Diane Dunbar requested an NDA and stated 'We'll need your standard NDA; please send the draft and we'll provide our full legal entity name, notice recipients, and signatory info,' signaling PBF runs a structured legal-review process. The NDA was sent (Zycus, Aug 2025), but PBF's legal details (entity name, recipients, signatory) were never provided, and the NDA status is unknown (likely stalled or deprioritized given the RFP took precedence). The RFP submission (22 Jun 2026) likely included standard terms and MSA expectations, but no SOW, MSA redline process, or InfoSec review timeline has been discussed. PBF's scale (3,800 employees, $2.5-3B spend, 1,285 suppliers) and the mention of IT coordination (Diane's title: Sr. Procurement Coordinator, IT) imply InfoSec and IT architecture review will be required, especially for the SAP integration (ECC today, S/4HANA future migration). No SI or partner involvement has been mentioned. The paper-process gap is that Zycus does not know: (1) whether PBF has preferred MSA terms or expects to use their paper, (2) InfoSec review timing and depth, (3) whether contracting can run in parallel with vendor selection or must wait until post-selection (likely the latter in a formal RFP). The immediate action is to surface this on the post-demo feedback call and request a procurement/legal process overview before commercials begin.

The process sequence visible to date is: intro/discovery call (Aug 2025, Diane Dunbar + Kelsey Mckell + Archland Bergeron), RFP issuance (early Jun 2026), Zycus intent-to-respond confirmed (2 Jun 2026), presales assigned, submission completed (22 Jun), and demos queued (Jul). decision_criteria.narrative: The RFP process itself signals structured evaluation criteria, with conference-room demos scheduled to begin ~13 Jul (SF Next Step 22 Jun 2026) and a formal submission deadline (22 Jun 5pm EDT). On the August 2025 intro call, Diane Dunbar named four priorities: (1) integration with the relevant stakeholder (current ERP) and future S/4HANA migration readiness, (2) supplier onboarding and management capabilities, (3) AI and automation to reduce manual work, and (4) spend visibility and compliance reporting. The RFP template request (Diane, Aug 2025: 'Can you send us a template outlining your key vendor questions, especially around supplier onboarding and AI capabilities?') reinforces AI and supplier management as evaluation focal points. PBF's scale (168K invoices, 1,285 suppliers, 2,335 contracts, 20 sourcing events/year with 10 complex) implies criteria will include transaction throughput, sourcing sophistication, and contract lifecycle management depth. However, the weighting of criteria, the scoring rubric, and how PBF will differentiate vendors in the conference-room demos remain undisclosed. The post-demo feedback call (week of 29 Jun) is the opportunity to clarify scoring, confirm whether AI/Merlin is a differentiator or table-stakes, and learn what gaps competitors exposed.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI fit signal is positive but unconfirmed. Diane Dunbar named 'AI and automation to reduce manual work' as a stated priority (Aug 2025 intro call: 'The team spends too much time on manual tasks that should be automated') and explicitly requested vendor questions 'especially around supplier onboarding and AI capabilities,' signaling AI is a formal RFP evaluation criterion. PBF's scale (168K invoices, 40-50K POs/PRs, 1,285 suppliers, 2,335 contracts, 20 sourcing events/year with 10 complex, 70 power users + 2,300 casual users) creates a strong use case for AI-driven automation (Merlin Intake for requisition automation, Agentic AI for supplier onboarding, ML iAnalyze for spend intelligence). However, the depth of AI appetite is unknown: is AI a nice-to-have (table-stakes checkbox in the RFP) or a must-have differentiator that will swing vendor selection? No AIS score or status is logged in Salesforce, and Zycus has not validated whether the AI story resonated in the August 2025 demo or how PBF is evaluating AI relative to other criteria (integration, cost, sourcing depth). The conference-room demos (~13 Jul) are the moment to test and confirm AI fit: position Merlin as the wedge that solves PBF's manual-work pain better than SAP (which lacks agentic AI) or any competitor, and gauge buyer reaction (enthusiasm, skepticism, or indifference). If AI lands as a differentiator, Zycus is AI Hungry; if it is treated as table-stakes, AI Curious.

PBF plans to migrate from the relevant stakeholder to S/4HANA 'in the coming years' (Diane Dunbar, Aug 2025 intro call), but no specific timeline disclosed. If the migration occurs mid-contract (e.g., 12-18 months post-go-live), Zycus will need to re-scope and re-integrate, potentially triggering budget overruns, project delays, or buyer dissatisfaction. SAP Ariba may position this as a risk ('why integrate with Zycus twice when SAP S/4 procurement is natively integrated?'). Proactive move: offer a phased integration roadmap (ECC today, S/4 migration support included in the contract at no incremental cost) to de-risk this and neutralize SAP's objection.

items.expected_effect: Neutralize SAP's integration objection, de-risk the S/4 migration uncertainty for PBF, and position Zycus as the vendor that solves both today's need (ECC integration) and tomorrow's (S/4 readiness) without incremental cost or project risk. This strengthens Zycus's competitive position and may become a key differentiator in vendor selection.

Diane requested 'a template outlining your key vendor questions, especially around supplier onboarding and AI capabilities' (Aug 2025), signaling AI is a decision criterion. value.detail: SAP S/4HANA migration timeline unclear, creating mid-contract re-integration risk. PBF plans to migrate from the relevant stakeholder to S/4HANA 'in the coming years' (Diane Dunbar, Aug 2025 intro call), but no specific timeline disclosed. If the migration occurs mid-contract (e.g., 12-18 months post-go-live), Zycus will need to re-scope and re-integrate, potentially triggering budget overruns, project delays, or buyer dissatisfaction. SAP Ariba may position this as a risk ('why integrate with Zycus twice when SAP S/4 procurement is natively integrated?'). Proactive move: offer a phased integration roadmap (ECC today, S/4 migration support included in the contract at no incremental cost) to de-risk this and neutralize SAP's objection.
